notes: [GENERIC EMAIL SCAN] From: Google <no-reply@accounts.google.com> | Persona: Uncle Ji (Mr. Khanna) | Reason: This message is a brand impersonation phishing attempt mimicking a Google Security Alert. It notifies the user about an 'App password' creation to create a sense of urgency and fear. This tactic is designed to trick the recipient into clicking a link to 'secure' their account, which typically leads to a credential harvesting site or a session-stealing page. Although the URLs appear to point to legitimate Google domains, the unsolicited nature and the request for immediate security action are key indicators of a phishing campaign.

[GENERIC EMAIL SCAN] From: Google <no-reply@accounts.google.com> | Persona: Siddharth | Reason: This message is a phishing attempt mimicking an official Google security notification. While it uses legitimate-looking URLs, the presence of a future copyright date (© 2026 Google LLC) is a common sign of a poorly constructed fraudulent template. The email employs a classic social engineering tactic by creating a sense of urgency regarding a password change to trick the recipient into engaging with the recovery link for credential harvesting.

[GENERIC EMAIL SCAN] From: Google <no-reply@accounts.google.com> | Persona: Mrs. Revathi | Reason: The message contains a future copyright date (2026), which is a common error in fraudulent phishing templates. It uses social engineering by claiming an account was recovered to create urgency, encouraging the recipient to click links that lead to credential harvesting or account takeover attempts.

[GENERIC EMAIL SCAN] From: System Design Course from SDIR <sdcourse+system-design-course-with-java-and@substack.com> | Persona: Mrs. Revathi | Reason: The message is an unsolicited marketing email for a paid course on Substack. It requests user action (clicking links) and money (purchasing a course with a discount), which meets the criteria for unsolicited solicitation in this security context.
